prompt: Cinematic wide-angle forest photograph, dusk light filtering through a canopy. The viewer stands among trees. Every tree immediately surrounding them is enormous — ancient, wide-trunked, their bark filling the frame. Yet when the eye travels outward toward the distance, the forest is clearly composed mostly of small, slender saplings. The dense ring of giants immediately around the viewer gives way to a sparse scatter of thin young trees stretching to the horizon. Both truths are simultaneously visible and unambiguous in the same frame: the viewer's immediate experience is dominated by giant trees, yet the overall forest is overwhelmingly made up of small ones. The local experience and the global distribution are in direct, visible contradiction. The viewer's presence implied by camera position distorts what feels representative. Standing here, you would never guess the true composition of this forest. Mood: quiet unease, something subtly wrong about what you're seeing without knowing why. Color palette: deep amber and sienna bark close up, cool blue-grey mist and pale green receding into distance. No text, no numbers, no labels, no charts, no diagrams. Photorealistic, not surreal or cartoonish. Single image, not a diptych.
visual_logic: A viewer surrounded by enormous trees, yet the forest beyond is mostly small saplings — what you experience when you arrive in the forest is unrepresentative of the whole distribution, because large trees dominate your local experience disproportionately.
